questions.scenario: Your 3/3 token is destroyed by combat damage. What happens to it?
choices: It stays in the graveyard like a normal creature
It goes to the graveyard, then ceases to exist as a state-based action
It is exiled instead of going to the graveyard
It returns to the token pile outside the game
questions.explanation: Tokens can go to the graveyard briefly, but state-based actions immediately remove them from the game. They cease to exist instead of staying in the graveyard.

questions.scenario: You cast Unsummon targeting your opponent's creature token. What happens?
choices: The token returns to its owner's hand
The token ceases to exist; it cannot exist in the hand
The token becomes a 0/0 and dies
questions.explanation: If a token would leave the battlefield for any zone other than the battlefield, it ceases to exist instead.

questions.scenario: Your opponent controls a 1/1 Soldier token. Can your Murder target it?
choices: No — tokens are not legal targets for destroy effects
Yes — tokens are permanents and can be targeted
Only if it is a legendary token
questions.explanation: Tokens are permanents on the battlefield. Destroy effects that target a creature can target tokens.

questions.scenario: You create a Treasure token on your turn. Nothing removes it. On your next turn, is it still on the battlefield?
choices: No — tokens are removed at end of turn
Yes — tokens remain until they leave the battlefield or the game ends
Only if you still control its creator
It becomes a 0/1 artifact until end of turn
questions.explanation: Tokens are permanents and persist across turns until an effect causes them to leave the battlefield.

questions.scenario: A spell creates a copy of a 2/2 Beast token. The copy is…
choices: A token (copies of tokens are tokens)
questions.explanation: If an effect creates a copy of a token, that copy is also a token.

questions.scenario: Your token is sacrificed to a cost. Does "whenever a creature dies" trigger?
choices: Yes — sacrificing a creature token makes it die, then it ceases to exist
Only if the token had a +1/+1 counter
questions.explanation: Sacrificing a token counts as it dying. Die triggers happen before state-based actions remove it from the graveyard.

questions.scenario: Can you cast Raise Dead targeting a creature token that died this turn?
choices: Yes — it was in the graveyard when it died
No — it ceases to exist in the graveyard and cannot be targeted there
Yes, but it enters as a 1/1
Only if the token was a nontoken copy
questions.explanation: Tokens cease to exist in the graveyard immediately. They are not valid targets for effects that retrieve creatures from the graveyard.

questions.scenario: You mill a card and a token creature card is revealed (in a format using token cards). What happens?
choices: The token enters the battlefield from the graveyard
Token cards in graveyards cease to exist; milling one has no lasting effect
You put the token into your hand
You create a token on the battlefield
questions.explanation: If a token would be put into any zone other than the battlefield, it ceases to exist instead.

questions.scenario: You control a 1/1 Soldier token. Can you activate an Equipment's equip ability targeting it?
choices: Yes — if it is a legal creature target
questions.explanation: Creature tokens are creatures. Equip can target them unless an effect says otherwise.

questions.scenario: You exile your creature token until end of turn, then it returns. What happens?
choices: It returns as the same object with the same counters
It ceases to exist when exiled; a new token is created when the effect tries to return it
questions.explanation: When a token leaves the battlefield, it ceases to exist. "Return" effects create a new token instead of returning the old one.

questions.scenario: You control Anointed Procession and create one 1/1 token. How many tokens do you get?
choices: Zero — Procession does not affect tokens
questions.explanation: Anointed Procession doubles tokens you create. One token becomes two.

questions.scenario: Which is true about tokens?
choices: Tokens are cards in all zones
Tokens are permanents on the battlefield but not cards
Tokens exist only in the command zone
Tokens are always colorless and not creatures
questions.explanation: Tokens are permanents on the battlefield but are not cards. Many effects care about "cards" vs "permanents."

questions.scenario: A spell costs "Sacrifice a creature." You control only a 1/1 token. Can you cast it?
choices: Yes — you may sacrifice the token to pay the cost
Only if the token has been on the battlefield since your last turn
questions.explanation: You may sacrifice a creature token to pay a sacrifice cost. The token then ceases to exist.

questions.scenario: You control a token with a +1/+1 counter. It dies. Later you cast Populate. What happens?
choices: Populate copies the dead token from the graveyard
Populate does nothing — there is no token on the battlefield with a +1/+1 counter to copy
Populate returns the token from the graveyard
questions.explanation: Populate puts a +1/+1 counter on a token you control. A dead token is gone and cannot be copied from the graveyard.

questions.scenario: You control two legendary tokens named "Marit Lage" (same name). What happens?
choices: Both stay — tokens ignore the legend rule
The legend rule applies; you choose one and the other ceases to exist when put into the graveyard
questions.explanation: The legend rule applies to legendary tokens. The one you put into the graveyard ceases to exist as a token.

questions.scenario: You cast Lightning Bolt on a 4/4. They cast Giant Growth (+3/+3) in response. Stack resolves top-down. Result?
choices: Bolt resolves first; creature dies before pump
questions.explanation: The response resolves first. Pump then bolt leaves a 4/7 with 3 damage marked.

questions.scenario: You cast a creature. Opponent casts Counterspell. Can you respond with another instant before Counterspell resolves?
choices: Yes — each player gets priority with spells on the stack
Only if you have no lands
questions.explanation: With spells on the stack, players receive priority in turn order and may cast instants or activate abilities.

questions.scenario: You cast a sorcery. Before it resolves, can your opponent cast an instant?
choices: No — sorcery speed locks the stack
Yes — they receive priority after you cast
Only if they have no creatures
questions.explanation: Casting a spell gives all players priority before it resolves.

questions.scenario: A spell resolves. What happens immediately afterward?
choices: Active player receives priority with an empty stack
questions.explanation: After a spell or ability resolves, the active player gets priority with the stack empty unless a new object was put on the stack.

questions.scenario: You cast a split second spell. Can opponents respond?
choices: No — players cannot cast spells or activate mana abilities while it is on the stack
Only if they have priority first
questions.explanation: Split second prevents other spells and non-mana abilities while the split second spell is on the stack.

questions.scenario: You cast Lightning Bolt on a 4/4. They cast Giant Growth (+3/+3) in response. Stack resolves top-down. Result?
choices: Bolt resolves first; creature dies before pump
questions.explanation: The response resolves first. Pump then bolt leaves a 4/7 with 3 damage marked.

questions.scenario: Your 2/2 first strike blocks their 4/4 without first strike. Damage?
choices: Yours deals 2 in first strike step; theirs deals 4 in normal step if yours lives
questions.explanation: First strike damage happens in the first combat damage step.

questions.scenario: Your 3/3 double strike is unblocked. Damage to player?
choices: 3 twice at different times only if blocked
questions.explanation: Double strike deals damage in both combat damage steps.

questions.scenario: 5/5 trample blocked by 3/3. Trample damage to player?
questions.explanation: Assign 3 lethal to blocker; remaining 2 tramples.

questions.scenario: 1/1 deathtouch trample blocked by 5/5. Trample over?
questions.explanation: Only 1 power to assign; 1 is lethal via deathtouch; none tramples.

questions.scenario: Your 4/4 lifelink deals 4 combat damage to a player. You…
questions.explanation: Lifelink gives life equal to damage dealt as it resolves.

questions.scenario: A 3/3 has 3 damage marked. No player passes. SBA check?
questions.explanation: Creatures with damage ≥ toughness are destroyed by SBA.

questions.scenario: You control two copies of the same legendary permanent. SBA?
choices: Choose one; others go to graveyard
questions.explanation: Legend rule is an SBA.

questions.scenario: A planeswalker has 0 loyalty. SBA?
questions.explanation: Planeswalkers with 0 loyalty go to graveyard.

questions.scenario: Indestructible 2/2 with 2 damage. Destroyed?
questions.explanation: Lethal damage is marked but indestructible prevents destruction.

questions.scenario: You have taken 21 combat damage from one commander this game. Result?
choices: You lose only if commander attacks alone
questions.explanation: 21 commander damage from one commander causes loss.

questions.scenario: You cast your commander from the command zone twice before. Additional cost?
questions.explanation: Commander tax is {2} per prior cast from command zone.

questions.scenario: Your commander is in the command zone. Is it a spell on the stack when you cast it?
choices: Yes — casting puts it on the stack like any creature spell
questions.explanation: Casting from command zone uses the stack normally.

questions.scenario: Commander damage counts…
choices: Only combat damage from that commander
questions.explanation: Commander damage tracks combat damage from that commander.

questions.scenario: You have taken 21 combat damage from one commander this game. Result?
choices: You lose only if commander attacks alone
questions.explanation: 21 commander damage from one commander causes loss.

questions.scenario: You cast your commander from the command zone twice before. Additional cost?
questions.explanation: Commander tax is {2} per prior cast from command zone.

questions.scenario: Your commander is in the command zone. Is it a spell on the stack when you cast it?
choices: Yes — casting puts it on the stack like any creature spell
questions.explanation: Casting from command zone uses the stack normally.

questions.scenario: Creature with hexproof. Opponent casts Murder targeting it.
questions.explanation: Hexproof prevents targeting by opponents.

questions.scenario: Your creature has shroud. Can you target it with your pump spell?
questions.explanation: Shroud prevents all targeting including yours.

questions.scenario: Opponent targets your creature with ward {2}. They decline to pay. Effect?
choices: The spell or ability is countered
questions.explanation: Ward counters unless the cost is paid when targeted.

questions.scenario: Creature with protection from black. Opponent casts black Murder.
choices: Cannot be targeted by black sources
questions.explanation: Protection from black prevents targeting from black sources.

questions.scenario: Permanent with "When ~ enters, draw a card" is cast. Trigger timing?
choices: When it enters the battlefield on resolution
questions.explanation: ETB triggers trigger on entering the battlefield.

questions.scenario: Token dies. "Whenever a creature dies, draw" and SBA order?
choices: Trigger goes on stack; token ceases to exist in graveyard via SBA
questions.explanation: Die triggers see the token die; then SBA remove it from the graveyard.

questions.scenario: Static ability "Creatures you control get +1/+1." When applies?
choices: Continuously while the source is on the battlefield
questions.explanation: Static abilities apply continuously in their layer.

questions.scenario: If damage would be prevented entirely, how much is marked?
choices: None — prevented damage is not marked
questions.explanation: Prevented damage is never marked.

questions.scenario: 2/2 flying blocked by 2/2 reach. Can it be blocked?
questions.explanation: Reach allows blocking creatures with flying.

questions.scenario: Menace creature attacked. One 2/2 blocker assigned.
questions.explanation: Menace cannot be blocked except by two or more creatures.

questions.scenario: Vigilance creature attacks. Can you tap it for an ability in same combat?
choices: Yes — vigilance does not tap to attack
questions.explanation: Vigilance means attacking does not cause it to tap.

questions.scenario: Creature with haste cast this turn. Can it attack?
choices: Yes — haste ignores summoning sickness for attack
questions.explanation: Haste allows attack and tap abilities despite control since this turn.

questions.scenario: Main phase, stack empty, land not played. May you play a land?
choices: Yes at sorcery speed in main phase
questions.explanation: Land drops are special actions at sorcery speed during main phase.

questions.scenario: Can you cast an instant during combat step?
questions.explanation: Instants can be cast any time you have priority including combat.

questions.scenario: Activated mana ability while a spell is on the stack.
questions.explanation: Mana abilities do not use the stack.

questions.scenario: You add mana to your pool during opponent's turn. When does it empty?
choices: When you pass priority unused at end of phase/step
questions.explanation: Mana empties from your pool at end of each step and phase when you pass priority and the stack is empty.

questions.scenario: Main phase, stack empty, land not played. May you play a land?
choices: Yes at sorcery speed in main phase
questions.explanation: Land drops are special actions at sorcery speed during main phase.

questions.scenario: Can you cast an instant during combat step?
questions.explanation: Instants can be cast any time you have priority including combat.

questions.scenario: Activated mana ability while a spell is on the stack.
questions.explanation: Mana abilities do not use the stack.

questions.scenario: A copy effect copies a creature token on the battlefield. The copy is…
questions.explanation: Copies of tokens are tokens.

questions.scenario: Clone enters copying a 3/2 creature. Its power and toughness are…
questions.explanation: Clone enters as a copy with copied characteristics.

questions.scenario: Two auras on same creature giving +2/+2 and -1/-1. Which applies last?
choices: Both always — use timestamp order in layer 7
questions.explanation: Continuous effects in the same layer use timestamp order unless dependency applies.
